name: speed-game (juego de velocidad) wiring
botUpdateServiceIntegrationNotes:
Where each piece goes in apps/bot/src/bot-update.service.ts (I did not edit this file myself — it's on the forbidden list):

1. Two import edits near the top of the file (~line 55-62 for the `import type {...} from "@superbot/data"` block, ~line 71-89 for the value-import block with the InMemory* defaults) and one import edit at ~line 621-672 (`import {...} from "./tokens.js"`). All three are simple alphabetical insertions, shown in botUpdateServiceSnippet.
2. Constructor: add the new `@Optional() @Inject(SPEED_GAME_REPOSITORY) private readonly speedGameRepository: SpeedGameRepository = new InMemorySpeedGameRepository()` param at the end of the constructor param list (~line 900, right before the closing `) {}`). I followed the exact convention of the *newest* constructor params (chatSettingRepository, guardianRepository, etc.) which use `@Optional()` + an in-memory default, rather than the older plain `@Inject(...)` (no default) style used by giveawayRepository et al. This matters here specifically: it means the feature works (against the transient in-memory store) even before tokens.ts/app.module.ts are updated, and typechecks/tests won't depend on that follow-up work landing first.
3. botHandlers(): add `{ name: "speed-game.command", handle: ({ context, update }) => this.handleSpeedGameCommand(context, update) }` right next to the two `giveaway.*` entries (~line 1396-1405) — this is a NEW dedicated handler+entry, not a new `case` inside the giant `handleUtilityPlusCommand` switch. Reasoning: handleUtilityPlusCommand's cases are dispatched by `update.command?.name` too, but every case I found there is a single-shot stateless (or single-repository-read) response; this feature needs its own injected repository (speedGameRepository) and a 3-command state machine (start/answer/force-close), which matches the "one repository-backed feature = one dedicated handler method + one botHandlers() entry" shape used for giveaway (handleGiveawayCommand/handleGiveawayJoin), not the giant switch's shape.
4. Add the two new private methods (`handleSpeedGameCommand`, `announceSpeedGameClose`) and the two new static members (`SPEED_GAME_WINDOW_MS`, `normalizeSpeedAnswer`) anywhere in the class body — I suggested right after `handleGiveawayJoin` (~line 7705) and next to `ADMIN_CACHE_TTL_MS` (~line 2004) respectively, but placement is not load-bearing.

Command-name / parser-layer gap check (explicitly requested): NONE FOUND — no edit needed anywhere else. I checked packages/telegram/src/parse-command.ts (`parseTelegramCommand`), which is the actual layer that turns raw Telegram text into `update.command = {name, args}`. It is a fully generic regex (`/^\/([a-z0-9_]+)(?:@bot)?(?:\s+(.*))?$/i`) with no allowlist of known command names — `/velocidad`, `/responder`, and `/cerrarvelocidad` will normalize into `update.command.name` correctly with zero changes there. I also confirmed (via grep) there is no separate global "known commands" registry anywhere else — every other domain (giveaway, economy, recipe, etc.) either does its own inline `update.command?.name === "..."` check inside its handler (the majority pattern) or, in giveaway's case, has its own small feature-scoped parser module (modules/community/src/giveaways.ts) that only that one handler imports — never a shared file. I followed the majority inline-check pattern rather than adding a new parser module, since it's simpler and this is a 3-command family, not a family large enough to warrant its own parser module. I also verified "responder" and "velocidad"/"cerrarvelocidad" don't collide with any existing top-level command name (there is an unrelated `/caza responder <texto>` *subcommand*, and an unrelated `"responder"` string literal used as an incident-event enum value — neither is a top-level `update.command.name`).

Two more shared files need a companion edit for the Prisma-backed store to actually run in production (both currently show `M` in `git status`, i.e. another process is already touching them mid-flight, so I did not edit them — the in-memory default from step 2 means the feature still typechecks/tests/runs without these, just non-durably):
- apps/bot/src/tokens.ts: append `export const SPEED_GAME_REPOSITORY = Symbol("SPEED_GAME_REPOSITORY");` (after the last line, `GUARDIAN_REPOSITORY`).
- apps/bot/src/app.module.ts: import `PrismaSpeedGameRepository` from "@superbot/data" and `SPEED_GAME_REPOSITORY` from "./tokens.js" (mirroring the existing `PrismaGiveawayRepository`/`GIVEAWAY_REPOSITORY` import lines), then add a provider block next to the Giveaway one:
  { provide: SPEED_GAME_REPOSITORY, useFactory: () => new PrismaSpeedGameRepository() },

Design/UX notes:
- Closing is lazy, not a persisted timer: there's no in-process setTimeout or worker/cron job scheduling an announcement N seconds later (grepped bot-update.service.ts — the only two setTimeout calls there are unrelated 2200ms delays, not a scheduling primitive; the established "close later" primitive in this codebase is the JobOutbox + worker-cron pattern used for retries/scheduled posts, which is out of scope for this task). Instead: the round carries `closesAt`; the round is actually marked closed and the winner announced either (a) by staff calling `/cerrarvelocidad`, or (b) automatically the next time anyone calls `/responder` or `/velocidad` after `closesAt` has passed. This means if nobody interacts with the chat again after the window elapses, the round is left "open" in storage forever with no proactive push message — acceptable for a live chat game (someone always eventually answers or an admin starts the next round) but worth flagging as a known gap if a guaranteed proactive announcement is required later (that would mean wiring this into the JobOutbox/worker cron, a materially bigger follow-up).
- `PrismaSpeedGameRepository.closeRound` is idempotent by design (re-running it on an already-closed round just recomputes the same deterministic ranking from the stored submissions and does not re-write `winnerUserId`), so both close triggers above can safely race/duplicate without corrupting state.
- Answer correctness is judged with a small accent/case-insensitive normalizer (`BotUpdateService.normalizeSpeedAnswer`, NFD strip + lowercase) applied at the bot-update.service.ts layer, not inside the repository — mirroring how `earnPoints` (economy) is pure logic invoked from bot-update.service.ts rather than from inside a repository.
- Verified: `node node_modules/typescript/bin/tsc -p packages/data/tsconfig.json --noEmit` shows exactly the expected class of error for speed-game-repository.ts — `TS2339: Property 'speedRound'/'speedRoundAnswer' does not exist on type 'PrismaClient'` — identical in kind to 5 other already-existing pending repositories in this same package (coop-city, eca-rule, known-admin, memory-sequence, quiz-difficulty), confirming this is the expected "written ahead of the schema.prisma edit" state, not a bug in my file. `node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run packages/data/src/speed-game-repository.test.ts` passes all 9 tests (InMemory implementation only, no Prisma involved).
risks: Low-risk, additive-only change: two brand-new files in packages/data/src (no existing file logic touched besides one appended export line in index.ts), plus text-only snippets/notes for the shared files I was told not to edit directly. No existing gate, permission check, or destructive action is removed or weakened — /velocidad and /cerrarvelocidad are admin/staff-gated via the existing isActorAdmin() helper (same gate used by dozens of other admin commands), and /responder is intentionally open to all users (it's an answer submission, not a destructive action). Main residual risk is the "no proactive timer" design choice (see notes): a round can stay open indefinitely if nobody sends /responder or /cerrarvelocidad after the window elapses — no data corruption results (closeRound is idempotent and safe to call late), but the group won't get an automatic "time's up" push without further work (JobOutbox/worker wiring), which was out of scope here. Also note PrismaSpeedGameRepository will not compile against the *generated* Prisma client until schema.prisma is updated with the model block and `prisma generate`/`db push` is re-run by whoever owns that file — expected and consistent with 5 sibling pending repositories already in the same state.
